title: RavenClaude dashboard
description: Your control panel for working with Claude Code under RavenClaude. Set what Claude may do on its own and see the safety checks every command passes through (Set up); review past decisions, live runs, perimeter alerts, and your security log (Look back); read plain-English explainers, command playbooks, and decision-tree guidance (Learn); and add plugins or wire RavenClaude into your tools (Install & help). The Settings page is where you tune permissions — Deny (never), Ask (check first), or Allow (go ahead) per action type, across three layers (User, Local, Project) where the strictest wins; it saves to .ravenclaude/comfort-posture.yaml and the /set-posture skill turns that into Claude Code's own .claude/settings.json rules.

layer_value.description: Per-layer value for a category. 'inherit' means this layer emits no rule and defers to Claude Code's default merge behaviour. Vocabulary: allow | ask | deny | inherit.

pattern_layer_object.description: Per-layer override for a single permission pattern. Each key is a settings layer; the value is allow | ask | deny | inherit. 'inherit' defers to the category-wide layer value for that pattern.

category_layer_object.description: v5 per-layer shape: each key is a settings layer. Omitted keys or 'inherit' values mean that layer emits no rule. Claude Code merges user + local + project at runtime; the strictest wins (deny > ask > allow). The optional `overrides` map fine-tunes individual permissions within the category, per layer.

overrides.description: Optional per-permission fine-tuning. Keys are exact permission patterns (e.g. "Bash(git push:*)"); each value is a per-layer object of allow | ask | deny | inherit. A non-inherit value overrides the category-wide layer value for that one permission.

category_overrides_object.description: Legacy v4 shape: a single default level with optional per-pattern overrides. Still accepted by the translator for backward compatibility.

overrides.description: Per-pattern overrides keyed by the exact Bash/Read/Write/etc. pattern string emitted by the translator. Use to tighten or relax specific patterns within the category. Example: { "Bash(rm:*)": "ask" }.

_level_descriptions.deny: Hard block. Claude refuses the action even if asked.

recommended._description: Applies the per-category recommended level. Reads inside the project flow; writes inside the project ask; everything that touches the outside world (git push, code exec, package install, web writes, files outside the project) asks. The 'we did the thinking for you' starting point — adjust individual rows from here.

deny._description: LOCKDOWN. Sets every category to DENY. Claude refuses every action in every category. Useful only in tightly-scoped reviews where you want Claude to read prose but take no action.

ask._description: Sets every category to ASK. Claude pauses to confirm before every non-trivial action. Maximum oversight, slowest velocity — a good starting point per Koch et al.'s 'OK blindness' finding; relax specific categories as you build trust.

allow._description: WARNING: Sets every category to ALLOW. Claude acts without asking on every pattern — INCLUDING git push, npm/pip install, arbitrary code execution (python -c), writes outside the project (~/.ssh, /etc), and POSTs to web endpoints. Use only inside throwaway sandboxes where damage can't propagate.

security_deny.title: Always-on security deny rules
security_deny.description: Patterns that are ALWAYS denied regardless of category levels. These survive every preset. The default list covers .env / .pem / credential reads and the most common destructive shell commands. Add or remove patterns in this list to change your security floor.

design_checkins.title: Design check-ins
design_checkins.description: Separate from the permission levels below. When ON, Claude pauses to confirm structural / architectural / design decisions (Keep / Update / Deny) before implementing — at ANY permission level, including Allow. When OFF (nonstop), Claude proceeds through those decisions using its best judgment and reports them afterward. The permission scale governs whether tool actions need a click; this governs whether design judgment is surfaced first. Honored by the Team Lead, which reads this flag at session start — it is a behavioral commitment, not a machine-enforced lock.
design_checkins.x-on-label: On — pause for design / architecture decisions
design_checkins.x-off-label: Off — nonstop; decide and report afterward

_category_value_shape.description: Documentation-only key (ignored by tooling). Each `categories.<name>` value can be (a) a plain string (v3/v4 legacy: single level for all layers), (b) an object `{ default, overrides }` (v4 per-pattern), or (c) an object `{ user, local, project }` with an optional `overrides` map (v5 per-layer, the canonical shape produced by the dashboard). Resolution: each layer independently resolves patterns (per-permission overrides win over the category-wide layer value); Claude Code merges user+local+project at runtime as deny > ask > allow.

_command_review_shape.description: Documentation-only key (ignored by tooling). Shape of the top-level `command_review:` block authored by the dashboard's Command-review panel and read by the command-review tribunal (the Thing) with higher precedence than .ravenclaude/thing.yaml. `panel` sets the per-seat model; `confidence_threshold` is the global fallback bar (also feeds decision-review); `gate_floor` is the headline comfort knob (enum medium | high | extreme, default high) that decides which risk tier and above is surfaced to the human after the tribunal clears it; `tiers` is the advanced per-tier override map. Tiers are exactly medium | high | extreme (the `low` tier runs no panel and is never emitted). Each tier carries `seats` (which of forseti | mimir | heimdall convene — thor is the tie-breaker and never appears here), `mandatory_seats` (a subset of `seats` that must vote), and a per-tier `confidence_threshold`. A deterministic high/critical concern can bump a command's base tier upward; the resolved tier selects the seats, the confidence bar, and whether a confident panel-ALLOW is surfaced to the human. `enabled` (bool, absent=true) is the master circuit-breaker AND-gated with every per-category toggle: false globally disables all tribunal review without changing per-category toggles; absent or true leaves per-category toggles in control. NOT a bulk setter — it is a top-level off-switch only; the self-disable and hard-rule floors still run whenever any category is toggled on. `dev_repo_exempt` (bool, absent=false, FAIL-SAFE OFF): when true, suppresses ONLY the substrate-path self-disable DENY for marketplace-substrate edits — allowing the plugin maintainer to edit hooks/ and scripts/ in the RavenClaude marketplace repo. Requires ALL three signals: (a) this flag is strictly true, (b) `gh repo view` resolves the authenticated repo to mcorbett51090/RavenClaude, (c) .claude-plugin/marketplace.json exists and parses with name 'ravenclaude'. Any error/missing gh/no auth/network failure/mismatch → exemption OFF (enforce). Does NOT weaken the hard-rule floor (force-push, curl|sh), the secret-egress backstop, xc.injection-attempt, or the discretionary panel.

global_default.title: Global default
global_default.description: Used for any category not explicitly set below. Apply a preset to set this and all categories at once.
global_default.x-rec-individual: Ask — solo work usually wants a moderate default; relax specific categories where you trust Claude more.
global_default.x-rec-team: Ask — collaborative work wants pauses in ambiguous places; keep the cautious default and grant Productive/Allow per category as needed.
global_default.x-controls: The fallback autonomy level for any category you don't set explicitly. Acts as a safety net so newly-added categories don't surprise you with a permissive default.
x-examples: If you set this to 'Ask' and don't touch any per-category row, every category behaves as Ask.
If you set this to 'Allow' but override `file_edit_global` to 'Deny', everything else is on allow — but Claude still refuses to write outside your project.
global_default.x-guidance: Most users start with 'Ask' here and relax specific categories where they trust Claude more. Setting this to 'Allow' is equivalent to enabling all categories at once — overrides become subtractive.

categories.title: Per-category overrides
categories.description: Each category's autonomy level. Override the global default per category.

file_read_project.title: Read files in this project
file_read_project.description: Reading source files inside the current project directory.
file_read_project.x-rec-individual: Allow — solo work benefits from Claude moving through your codebase freely; reads are essentially free.
file_read_project.x-rec-team: Allow — same as solo; reading shared code doesn't affect teammates and is cheap regardless.
file_read_project.x-controls: Anything Claude does to LOOK at files inside your current project directory — opening, scanning, reading. No changes to disk, no shell side effects, no network calls. This is the cheapest category.
x-examples: Opening a file in the IDE
Walking the directory tree to understand the project layout
file_read_project.x-guidance: Reading source files is essentially free — Claude can't break anything by looking. Most users keep this on 'Allow'. Tighten to 'Ask' or 'Deny' only if your project has files Claude shouldn't routinely scan (a checked-in keystore, a vendored secret, a generated SBOM with binary blobs).

file_edit_project.title: Edit files in this project
file_edit_project.description: Creating or modifying files inside the current project.
file_edit_project.x-rec-individual: Allow — solo work can rely on git for review; let Claude refactor and generate freely, then look at the diff.
file_edit_project.x-rec-team: Ask — teammates will be reviewing or blocked-on the changes; pausing on each edit preserves the review handoff and keeps surprises small.
file_edit_project.x-controls: Write, Edit, MultiEdit — anything Claude does to CHANGE a file inside your current project. The change is recoverable via git as long as you commit regularly, but it does affect your working tree immediately.
x-examples: Generating a new test file from a spec
Adding documentation to an existing module
file_edit_project.x-guidance: If you want to review every change before it lands, set this to 'Ask'. If you trust Claude to refactor or generate freely and rely on git for review, 'Allow' works — the diff is visible after the fact. Pair with 'Ask' on shell_remote_mutate so Claude can edit freely but still pauses before pushing.

file_read_global.title: Read files outside this project
file_read_global.description: Reading anything outside the current project directory.
file_read_global.x-rec-individual: Ask — even solo, you don't want Claude reading ~/.ssh or ~/.aws without a deliberate prompt.
file_read_global.x-rec-team: Ask — same as solo; secrets outside the project are always a deliberate decision regardless of who you work with.
file_read_global.x-controls: Anything Claude does to READ files that live OUTSIDE your current project — your home directory, system paths, other projects. These paths frequently contain secrets or configuration Claude has no business with.
x-examples: cat ~/.ssh/config — SSH keys and known hosts
Reading from another project's directory tree
file_read_global.x-guidance: Almost always set to 'Ask' or 'Deny'. Files outside your project often contain secrets — SSH keys, AWS creds, browser cookies, ~/.netrc. Only relax this if you have a specific reason (e.g., a single global config the agent legitimately needs to consult).

file_edit_global.title: Edit files outside this project
file_edit_global.description: Writing to your home directory or system paths.
file_edit_global.x-rec-individual: Ask — solo work that wants to touch ~/.gitconfig or a global setting is the exception, not the rule. Force the pause.
file_edit_global.x-rec-team: Ask — same as solo; global writes are infrequent enough that always asking is the safe default.
file_edit_global.x-controls: Anything Claude does to CHANGE files outside your current project — home directory, system config, other projects. These changes are often unrecoverable (no git tracking) and can affect every other workflow on your machine.
x-examples: Write('~/.profile', ...) — adding to your shell init
Writing into /etc/ (usually requires sudo, separate concern)
Touching another project's files from this session
file_edit_global.x-guidance: Keep this on 'Ask' or 'Deny' unless you have a specific reason. CVE-2026-39861 is the canonical example of what goes wrong when sandboxed code escapes the workspace and writes outside. The few cases where you DO want global edits (e.g., updating ~/.gitconfig) are infrequent enough that asking is fine.

shell_readonly.title: Read-only shell commands
shell_readonly.description: ls, cat, git status, grep, find. Cheap shell with no side effects.
shell_readonly.x-rec-individual: Allow — cheap shell is cheap regardless of context; let it run freely.
shell_readonly.x-rec-team: Allow — same as solo; read-only commands can't affect teammates' state.
shell_readonly.x-controls: Shell commands that DON'T change anything on disk and DON'T talk to a server. The 'cheap' shell — running them and not running them produces the same world afterward.
x-examples: git status, git log, git diff
shell_readonly.x-guidance: Claude Code already auto-allows many of these via its built-in READONLY_COMMANDS list, so the prompt rate is already low. 'Allow' is usually right — 'never ask for cheap shell' is the most common preference. Tighten only if you want every command logged into the prompt history.

shell_local_mutate.title: Local shell mutations
shell_local_mutate.description: mkdir, mv, cp, git commit. Changes filesystem locally.
shell_local_mutate.x-rec-individual: Allow — solo work benefits from Claude restructuring and committing locally; git history catches anything that goes wrong.
shell_local_mutate.x-rec-team: Ask — even local mutations (git checkout switching branches, mkdir reorganizing things) can affect what teammates see if they touch the wrong file later. Pausing on local mutations is cheap insurance.
shell_local_mutate.x-controls: Shell commands that CHANGE your local filesystem but DON'T talk to a server. Effects stay on your machine and are usually recoverable (git reflog exists, files can be restored from snapshots).
x-examples: rm temp/scratch.txt (recoverable from trash on most OSes)
shell_local_mutate.x-guidance: Local mutations are usually recoverable — git history catches deletes, files can be restored. 'Ask' or 'Allow' is the typical choice. Tighten if you're working in a directory where moves/deletes would be hard to recover (e.g., a folder Claude shouldn't restructure without confirmation).

shell_remote_mutate.title: Remote shell mutations
shell_remote_mutate.description: git push, gh pr create, npm publish — anything that touches a shared system.
shell_remote_mutate.x-rec-individual: Ask — even solo, the audit-trail value of confirming each push is worth the friction.
shell_remote_mutate.x-rec-team: Ask — pushes notify teammates, trigger CI, may break their working state. Every one deserves an explicit click.
shell_remote_mutate.x-controls: Shell commands that PUBLISH to a system other people can see — git remotes, GitHub Issues/PRs, package registries, cloud buckets. Once these complete, you can't simply 'undo' them; reversal usually requires a counter-action that's also visible.
x-examples: gh pr comment (visible to everyone in the repo)
aws s3 sync ... s3://bucket/ (changes remote state)
shell_remote_mutate.x-guidance: Most users keep this on 'Ask' to preserve the review step. The cost of a wrong push isn't just the bad commit — it's the explanation to teammates, the revert PR, the audit trail. Even when Claude is 'usually right,' the asymmetric cost makes confirming each push worth it.

shell_code_exec.title: Arbitrary code execution
shell_code_exec.x-rec-individual: Ask — arbitrary code exec is the shortest path to RCE if a prompt goes sideways. Worth pausing on every one.
shell_code_exec.x-rec-team: Ask — same as solo; the risk doesn't change with team size.
shell_code_exec.x-controls: Any command that lets Claude run code that WASN'T pre-written. These bypass per-tool gating because the inner code can do anything the shell can do.
shell_code_exec.x-guidance: This is the category most often abused in security incidents — Cursor's settings.json injection (GHSA-ff64-7w26-62rf) ended in arbitrary `python -c` execution via SessionStart hooks. 'Ask' is the safe default. Don't relax this unless you have a specific reason (e.g., a sandboxed REPL where exec is the whole point).

shell_package_install.title: Package installs
shell_package_install.description: npm install, pip install, apt install, brew install.
shell_package_install.x-rec-individual: Ask — supply-chain typosquats are the most common attack vector; verify the package name once per install.
shell_package_install.x-rec-team: Ask — same as solo; package installs are usually rare enough that asking is cheap.
shell_package_install.x-controls: Commands that download and install packages from a registry. Package installs are higher-risk than they appear: many run post-install scripts with full shell access on your machine.
shell_package_install.x-guidance: Most users keep this on 'Ask' so you can review the package name and source before it lands. Supply-chain attacks (typosquats, malicious updates of legitimate packages) are real — the request to install something is the right place to pause.

network_read.title: Web reads
network_read.description: WebFetch on known documentation domains; HTTP GETs.
network_read.x-rec-individual: Allow — Claude needs to look things up to give grounded answers; reads are low-risk.
network_read.x-rec-team: Allow — same as solo; reading public docs doesn't affect teammates.
network_read.x-controls: GET-style requests that READ from the web. No mutation, no side effects on the remote system. Risk is limited to what Claude does with the response (which it has to read carefully anyway).
x-examples: Querying a CDN for a library's docs
network_read.x-guidance: 'Allow' is usually right — Claude needs to look things up to give grounded answers. Tighten to 'Ask' only if you're worried about data exfiltration via URL parameters (e.g., Claude embedding sensitive strings in a GET) — rare but possible.

network_write.title: Web writes
network_write.description: POST, PUT, DELETE — anything that mutates remote state.
network_write.x-rec-individual: Ask — POSTs have side effects (charged API calls, posted messages, triggered workflows). Even solo, the asymmetric cost makes pausing worth it.
network_write.x-rec-team: Ask — webhooks may notify teammates or trigger their workflows; pausing is doubly important.
network_write.x-controls: HTTP methods that CHANGE state on the remote system. These have side effects ranging from 'charged my API account' to 'posted a message to a Slack channel' to 'triggered a production deployment.'
x-examples: Posting to a Slack incoming webhook
network_write.x-guidance: Keep this on 'Ask' unless you're operating in a sandbox where remote writes are explicitly part of the workflow (e.g., a test environment with disposable resources). The 'I clicked the wrong button' cost is asymmetric — confirming each one is cheap.

mcp_tools.title: MCP server tools
mcp_tools.description: Tools provided by connected MCP servers.
mcp_tools.x-rec-individual: Ask — MCP tool trust is best done server-by-server; the global default should ask, then you pin trusted servers individually in your Claude Code config.
mcp_tools.x-rec-team: Ask — same as solo; the global default is conservative; per-server trust is where the team-vs-solo distinction actually matters.
mcp_tools.x-controls: The DEFAULT autonomy level applied to any MCP (Model Context Protocol) server you've connected. Each server can also be configured individually in your Claude Code config; this is the fallback for servers without specific trust settings.
x-examples: uber.get_estimates(...) — third-party API, makes external calls
filesystem_mcp.read_file(...) — depends on what path it can see
mcp_tools.x-guidance: MCP trust is best done server-by-server, not globally. Keep this on 'Ask' as a safety net and configure trusted servers individually in your Claude Code config. A blanket 'Allow' here means any MCP server you add automatically gets full autonomy — usually not what you want.
